title: A Converse to a Theorem of Gross-Zaqier-Kolyvagin
speaker: Christopher Skinner
affiliation: Princeton University; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: The theorem of the title is that if the L-function L(E,s) of an elliptic curve E over the rationals vanishes to order r=0 or 1 at s=1 then the rank of the group of rational rational points of E equals r and the Tate-Shafarevich group of E is finite. This talk will describe an approach to the converse. The methods are mostly p-adic.

proof_stages.label: BSD and Gross-Zagier-Kolyvagin context
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with elliptic curves over Q, the BSD prediction relating Mordell-Weil rank to the order of vanishing of L(E,s) at s=1, the Gross-Zagier-Kolyvagin rank-zero and rank-one implications, and the desire for a rank-one converse.

proof_stages.label: Selmer-theoretic formulation and Theorem A
proof_stages.summary: The expected rank-one converse is reformulated in terms of the p-infinity Selmer group having corank one and a surjective localization map at p, leading to Theorem A under squarefree-conductor, ordinary, residual irreducibility, and ramification hypotheses, with mod-p Selmer conditions giving a checkable substitute.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to a non-torsion Heegner point
proof_stages.summary: The proof must manufacture a rational point, so an imaginary quadratic field K is chosen and the Gross-Zagier factorization is used to reduce the theorem to proving that a suitable Heegner point P_K is non-torsion, equivalently that its local formal logarithm is nonzero.

proof_stages.label: BDP formula for the Heegner logarithm
proof_stages.summary: After defining the formal logarithm and an anticyclotomic p-adic L-function for E/K, the Bertolini-Darmon-Prasanna formula identifies the p-adic L-value at the trivial character with a nonzero scalar times log(P_K)^2, motivated by Waldspurger's torus-period formula and Coleman integration.

proof_stages.label: Main-conjecture implication and Selmer vanishing
proof_stages.summary: A one-sided Greenberg main-conjecture type result gives the implication that vanishing of the relevant p-adic L-value would force a certain nonstandard Selmer cohomology group over K to be nonzero, while the hypotheses of Theorem A and the choice of K force that group to vanish, proving the needed p-adic nonvanishing.

proof_stages.label: Automorphic proof of the implication
proof_stages.summary: The key p-adic-L/Selmer implication is proved by transferring the modular form attached to E to a definite unitary group, constructing Eisenstein series on U(3,1), showing congruences to cuspidal families via constant-term and Fourier-Jacobi coefficient computations, and invoking nonvanishing results; the restrictive conductor hypotheses enter mainly in these local coefficient computations.

proof_stages.label: Converse theorem and density applications
proof_stages.summary: Theorem A yields the stated Gross-Zagier-Kolyvagin converse for semistable curves by choosing a suitable ordinary prime p, and the mod-p Selmer criterion together with Bhargava-Shankar equidistribution gives positive-proportion consequences once the local hypotheses are weakened, with final discussion of proportions and small-prime issues.

examples.name: CM elliptic curves in the rank-zero converse
examples.note: CM elliptic curves are cited only as a historical special case where the rank-zero converse was first proved, illustrating the background rather than driving the argument.
examples.engagement_reason: CM elliptic curves are only cited as the earlier known rank-zero converse case due to Greenberg, with no computation or construction performed on them.

examples.name: Additive reduction at 2 as a sample weakening
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker uses the case of allowing additive reduction at 2 to illustrate how weakening the local hypotheses would feed into the positive-proportion application.
examples.engagement_reason: additive reduction at 2 is presented as a hypothetical weakening of the hypotheses that would imply a positive-proportion theorem, but no specific curve or verification is worked through.

examples.name: Small primes for computable Selmer conditions
examples.note: The primes p=2,3,5 are mentioned as concrete settings where mod-p Selmer conditions can be counted or compared with Bhargava-Shankar methods; this illustrates computability of the criterion rather than producing the proof idea.
examples.engagement_reason: small primes such as 2, 3, and 5 are discussed as primes where Selmer conditions might be counted or where technical issues arise, but no Selmer computation is carried out for any of them.
